title: SAAG Calculator – Serum Ascites Albumin Gradient
tagline: Calculate the serum-ascites albumin gradient to distinguish portal hypertension from other causes of ascites at the bedside.
intro: Enter serum and ascitic fluid albumin values in g/dL to compute SAAG and view the standard diagnostic interpretation.
formula: Formula: SAAG = serum albumin − ascites albumin. A gradient of 1.1 g/dL or more suggests portal hypertension; a lower gradient suggests a non-portal hypertensive cause.

error.invalid: Enter valid serum and ascites albumin values in g/dL.

category.portal_hypertension: SAAG ≥ 1.1 g/dL — portal hypertension / transudative pattern
category.non_portal: SAAG < 1.1 g/dL — non-portal hypertension / exudative pattern

about.heading: About the SAAG calculator
about.body:
The serum-ascites albumin gradient, usually abbreviated SAAG, is one of the most useful laboratory tools for evaluating new ascites. Instead of focusing on the total protein concentration alone, SAAG compares the albumin concentration in serum with the albumin concentration in ascitic fluid. This gradient reflects portal venous pressure more reliably than older transudate-versus-exudate rules. In practical bedside medicine, it helps clinicians quickly separate ascites related to portal hypertension from ascites caused by malignancy, infection, pancreatic disease, or other non-portal processes. Because both values are routinely available after paracentesis and serum testing, the calculation is straightforward and highly relevant in hepatology, gastroenterology, emergency medicine, and inpatient care.

The threshold that matters most is 1.1 g/dL. A SAAG of 1.1 or greater strongly suggests portal hypertension, meaning the driving force behind the ascites is elevated hydrostatic pressure within the portal circulation. Cirrhosis is the classic cause, but heart failure, Budd-Chiari syndrome, and massive hepatic metastases can also produce a high gradient. A SAAG below 1.1 points away from portal hypertension and raises concern for causes such as peritoneal carcinomatosis, tuberculous peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatic ascites, or secondary bacterial and inflammatory processes. The result does not by itself make the diagnosis, but it narrows the differential meaningfully.

Timing and sampling quality matter when interpreting SAAG. Serum and ascitic albumin should ideally be measured close together because large shifts in plasma volume or treatment effects can alter the comparison. Clinical context is also essential. A patient with a high SAAG and low ascitic protein may have uncomplicated cirrhosis, while a patient with a high SAAG and high protein may fit more with cardiac ascites or hepatic venous outflow obstruction. Additional tests such as cell count, differential, culture, cytology, total protein, amylase, triglycerides, and imaging often provide the next diagnostic layer once the gradient is known.

This calculator is useful for confirming the arithmetic instantly and reinforcing how the 1.1 g/dL threshold guides differential diagnosis. Still, it should be used as a decision-support aid rather than a replacement for formal interpretation. Management of ascites depends on the overall clinical picture, including liver function, renal status, suspicion for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and signs of malignancy or heart failure. Always integrate SAAG with history, examination, and the rest of the paracentesis data before making treatment decisions.

examples.heading: SAAG examples
examples.intro: Load common ascites scenarios to compare portal hypertensive and non-portal hypertensive patterns.

items.input: Serum albumin 3.4 g/dL, ascites albumin 1.8 g/dL
items.note: Supports portal hypertension, as in cirrhosis or heart failure.

items.input: Serum albumin 2.8 g/dL, ascites albumin 2.2 g/dL
items.note: Suggests a non-portal cause such as infection or malignancy.

items.input: Serum albumin 4.0 g/dL, ascites albumin 3.0 g/dL
items.note: Just below the standard threshold, so broader clinical review is important.

howto.heading: How to use the SAAG calculator
steps: Obtain the serum albumin level and the matching ascitic fluid albumin level in g/dL.
Enter both values into the calculator exactly as reported by the lab.
Click Calculate SAAG to subtract ascites albumin from serum albumin.
Interpret the result against the 1.1 g/dL threshold and combine it with the rest of the paracentesis workup.

items.q: What does a high SAAG mean?
items.a: A SAAG of 1.1 g/dL or greater points toward portal hypertension as the mechanism behind ascites. Cirrhosis is the most common cause, but cardiac ascites and hepatic venous outflow obstruction can also produce a high gradient.

items.q: What does a low SAAG mean?
items.a: A SAAG below 1.1 g/dL suggests that portal pressure is not the main driver of fluid accumulation. Malignancy, infection, pancreatic disease, and other inflammatory or peritoneal causes should be considered.

items.q: Why is SAAG preferred over classifying ascites by protein alone?
items.a: SAAG correlates better with portal pressure and more accurately separates portal hypertensive ascites from other etiologies. Total protein still helps, but on its own it is less reliable for the main diagnostic split.

items.q: Can ascites albumin ever be higher than serum albumin?
items.a: It can happen because of measurement timing, laboratory variation, or unusual clinical situations. The calculator will still subtract the values, but unexpected results should prompt review of specimen timing and the broader clinical context.

items.q: Is SAAG enough to diagnose spontaneous bacterial peritonitis?
items.a: No. SBP is diagnosed primarily with ascitic neutrophil count and culture data, not SAAG. The gradient helps with etiology of ascites, while infection requires separate evaluation and urgent treatment if suspected.
